name: Belgian UFO Wave
description: The Belgian UFO Wave refers to a series of alleged UFO sightings that occurred in Belgium between November 1989 and April 1990. It is considered one of the most extensively documented cases of mass UFO sightings. Proponents claim that thousands of people witnessed triangular or delta-shaped objects moving silently and slowly across the Belgian skies. The phenomenon was even investigated by the Belgian military, who released a detailed report concluding that conventional explanations could not account for all the sightings. Key evidence cited includes numerous eyewitness accounts, photographic evidence (though often of questionable quality), and radar tracks. The most famous photograph depicts a triangular object with three lights, though its authenticity remains debated. Mainstream skepticism focuses on the possibility of misidentification of conventional aircraft (such as stealth aircraft undergoing testing), mass hysteria fueled by media attention, and hoaxes. The official military report, while acknowledging unexplained phenomena, did not definitively conclude the objects were extraterrestrial. Critics point out the lack of high-quality, verifiable photographic or video evidence and suggest that psychological factors played a significant role in the widespread reporting of similar sightings. The wave remains a subject of debate, with some viewing it as compelling evidence of extraterrestrial visitation and others as a case study in the power of suggestion and the challenges of eyewitness testimony.
subtitle: Mass UFO sightings over Belgium.

claims.claim: Large, silent triangular UFOs were observed.
claims.evidence: Thousands of eyewitness reports describe similar objects.
claims.criticism: Eyewitness accounts are subjective and prone to error or misinterpretation, especially under stress or in low-light conditions. Group suggestibility likely amplified the phenomenon.

claims.claim: Radar tracked objects moving at improbable speeds and maneuvers.
claims.evidence: Belgian Air Force radar data showed unidentified objects performing unusual movements.
claims.criticism: Radar anomalies can be caused by atmospheric conditions, equipment malfunctions, or misinterpretation of data. Civilian radar confirmation is lacking.

claims.claim: The Belgian military investigated and could not explain all the sightings.
claims.evidence: The Belgian Air Force released a report acknowledging unexplained aerial phenomena.
claims.criticism: The report did not conclude the objects were extraterrestrial, only that they could not be definitively identified as conventional aircraft or natural phenomena. Unidentified does not equate to extraterrestrial.

claims.claim: Photographic evidence exists to support the sightings.
claims.evidence: Several photographs and videos were taken of the alleged UFOs.
claims.criticism: Most photographic and video evidence is low-quality and easily explainable as hoaxes, misidentifications of conventional objects, or digital artifacts. The most famous triangular photo remains of questionable authenticity.

keyFigures.name: General Wilfried De Brouwer
keyFigures.role: Chief of Operations of the Belgian Air Force during the wave, oversaw the investigation.

keyFigures.name: Renaud Leclet
keyFigures.role: Ufologist who extensively researched and promoted the Belgian UFO Wave.

keyFigures.name: August Meessen
keyFigures.role: Professor of physics who investigated the photographic evidence and raised concerns about its authenticity.

mythologyConnections: Airborne deities and celestial chariots in various mythologies
Modern interpretations of angels as extraterrestrial visitors

timeline.event: First widely reported sightings of triangular UFOs in Belgium.
The Belgian Air Force attempts to intercept the UFOs, but fails.
General De Brouwer publicly discusses the Belgian UFO Wave, further fueling public interest.
The Belgian UFO Wave continues to be debated and analyzed by ufologists and skeptics.

sources.author: Belgian Air Force
